raw: {"weights":{"R1.1":0.7,"R1.2":0.7,"R1.3":0.7,"R1.4":0.7,"R1.5":0.3,"R1.6":0.3,"R1.7":0.7,"RX.1":1.0,"RX.2":1.0,"RX.3":1.0,"RX.4":1.0,"RX.5":1.0},"active_set":["R1.1","R1.2","R1.3","R1.4","R1.7","RX.1","RX.2","RX.3","RX.4","RX.5"],"rationale":{"R1.1":"The artifact makes an explicit contribution claim (hybrid index achieving in-memory recall at compressed-method storage costs) plus an implicit gap statement; any credible review must verify that the claim is clearly bounded and genuinely supported by the section's framing.","R1.2":"The section chains a premise sequence—prior methods' individual limitations → FastNav's design choices → a production-viability conclusion—and cross-section coherence (e.g., anisotropic compression attributed to ScaNN yet re-claimed as FastNav's contribution) must be checked for logical gaps.","R1.3":"A specific quantitative claim (1.3× speedup over 'strongest prior baseline' at matched recall@10) is asserted without an accompanying table or figure in the artifact; a review must flag whether this claim is adequately anchored to visible evidence or requires a forward reference.","R1.4":"FastNav is positioned as novel by combining anisotropic quantization (ScaNN) with disk-resident traversal (DiskANN); the delta over trivial method fusion is not argued, making novelty assessment a central review responsibility.","R1.7":"Five papers are cited with specific author lists and years (Indyk & Motwani 1998, Jégou et al. 2011, Guo et al. 2020, Subramanya et al. 2019, Fu et al. 2019); accuracy and coverage of these attributions are directly verifiable and errors would materially undermine the related-work framing."},"clarification_needed":true,"clarification_question":"Should this review prioritize technical and scientific validity (soundness of the novelty claim, adequacy of the 1.3× speedup evidence, citation accuracy) or editorial quality (prose clarity, sentence-level polish, paragraph flow)?"}

rationale.R1.1: The artifact makes an explicit contribution claim (hybrid index achieving in-memory recall at compressed-method storage costs) plus an implicit gap statement; any credible review must verify that the claim is clearly bounded and genuinely supported by the section's framing.
rationale.R1.2: The section chains a premise sequence—prior methods' individual limitations → FastNav's design choices → a production-viability conclusion—and cross-section coherence (e.g., anisotropic compression attributed to ScaNN yet re-claimed as FastNav's contribution) must be checked for logical gaps.
rationale.R1.3: A specific quantitative claim (1.3× speedup over 'strongest prior baseline' at matched recall@10) is asserted without an accompanying table or figure in the artifact; a review must flag whether this claim is adequately anchored to visible evidence or requires a forward reference.
rationale.R1.4: FastNav is positioned as novel by combining anisotropic quantization (ScaNN) with disk-resident traversal (DiskANN); the delta over trivial method fusion is not argued, making novelty assessment a central review responsibility.
rationale.R1.7: Five papers are cited with specific author lists and years (Indyk & Motwani 1998, Jégou et al. 2011, Guo et al. 2020, Subramanya et al. 2019, Fu et al. 2019); accuracy and coverage of these attributions are directly verifiable and errors would materially undermine the related-work framing.

parsed.clarification_question: Should this review prioritize technical and scientific validity (soundness of the novelty claim, adequacy of the 1.3× speedup evidence, citation accuracy) or editorial quality (prose clarity, sentence-level polish, paragraph flow)?
